channel.title: Comments for page "Do You Wish You Could Disappear?"
channel.description: Posts in the discussion thread "Do You Wish You Could Disappear?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You two made quite the interesting mini-discussion here, so thanks for all the information from both of you.

Mostly I'm inclined to agree that, yes, them getting away with kidnapping a few hundred people over a few days is unrealistic in the same way that most of what they get away with is unrealistic. That being said, I was thinking as I wrote that taking people from law enforcement and government backgrounds makes for a somewhat excusable disappearance reason ("oh, the whole family knew they were in a risky field") but probably not to a degree of complete realism.

Of course, an implication I've always liked is that we don't know what's realistic or not in terms of who the Foundation can take off the radar without anyone noticing. They might do it so well that people get expunged from missing persons cases altogether.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You have a lot of good points here.

+ show block
– hide block

If they're getting D-class by kidnapping, they'd have to come from the very fringes of society (an idea I'm fond of because it adds to the horror of the whole premise). Most anyone with an account on this site is too on-the-grid to disappear like that, so it makes sense that less realistic D-class backgrounds would be more relatable to writers and therefore show up a lot.

I can justify to myself that for the occasional job that needs a special skill set, they would resort to yanking a surgeon or mathematician or retired FBI agent into a van, investigation be damned - they can quash it if it somehow gets close to them. People from those backgrounds do go missing too, occasionally, and the Foundation would be able to target ones who have marital/legal/financial problems as a smokescreen and no loved ones to search for them.

I agree that most D-class would think they were actually doing something else - I imagine a lot of arrangements like the ones in heart in the hand of matter or boxes. I also like the idea that there are dentist's offices or national parks or highway rest stops where people just like you and me are grabbed for quick tests and then amnesticized and released, with their death made to look like an accident if something goes wrong.

Like you said, none of it is truly realistic, and it all comes down to individual headcanon. I agree that the Foundation would put D-class into dangerous situations only when necessary - I just also imagine that the Foundation's world is so hostile that "only when necessary" still requires kidnapping and human sacrifice on a massive scale.

For what it's worth, that NCIC report said that over 99% of missing children are found in the first few hours, so that's nice to know.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The majority (>400,000) of the people reported missing are minors, though, and in almost everyone's writing and headcanon that excludes them from being D-class except in very specific circumstances.

I agree that there are still a large number of missing persons who are never found and would theoretically be able to be kidnapped by a fictional shadowy organisation for experiments without anyone noticing. However, they disproportionately belong to marginalised groups (homeless people, substance users, people with severe chronic mental illness, etc.), since it's an unfortunate aspect of society that people are more likely to expect them to go missing and they get investigated less.

The problem is that people rarely write these groups as kidnapped civilian D-class - the subjects of these stories are usually 'ordinary' middle-class people with stable jobs, lives and personal relationships, I suppose because to most authors they are more relatable. In every country in the world when someone like that disappears there is a massive media circus and people never stop talking about it. This tale in particular stretches that further as the disappeared people are from a small geographical area and mostly with similar backgrounds, which people would be even more likely to notice - though it serves a narrative purpose here and like I said I think this is good quality enough to overlook it.

As an aside, although applying consistent logic to the Foundation is a fool's errand, I personally think the D-class situation doesn't work with either prisoners or missing persons, and that any 'real' Foundation would have to use volunteers, who I imagine would be recruited and employed just like any other volunteers for a research study (albeit a shady one) and leave the Foundation's employ thinking they participated in a totally normal study, perhaps with the help of amnestics, with the truly 'disposable' personnel from prisoners or missing persons being reserved for SCPs that require a person to be fed to them regularly to save the world or whatever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Funnily enough a lot of my dreams end up being the same, where the faster I try to run the slower I go. It's quite upsetting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
According to the FBI's National Crime Information Center, 63,515 of the ~650,000 people reported missing in the USA in 2017 alone still haven't been found.

The most recent.Bureau of Justice Statistics report I could find was 2014, which showed that 3,927 people were reported dead within the American prison system that year. However, all of their deaths are carefully documented, and in general the whole reason to put people in jail is so they'll be watched all the time. It would be much harder to cover up the disappearance of prisoners than to, say, set up Tindr accounts under fake identities so agents can take candidates on a couple dates to earn their trust, then get them in a car and drive them right over to the nearest Site for processing. Or just go get the person from their apartment under threat of force, if they fill some specific requirement like the one in the story.

The vast majority of the 2.3 million people incarcerated in the USA are there for relatively minor stuff such as drug possession - most of us would agree those aren't crimes that merit enslavement and horrible death, so it's not like taking people from jails is really much better from a moral standpoint anyway.

By the way, according to the BJS report, about 20 people per year actualy do disappear from the prison system and are never seen again, so that's unsettling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was gonna call this a "not my headcanon, but still good" kind of upvote, but that final section ties everything together and it's great. :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Damn, that ending though… It's like a written nightmare, where you feel the adrenaline and you run as fast as you can, but it's just not fast enough. Very well written, Cyantreuse!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I find it hard to suspend disbelief to accept the Civilian Use Protocol as it comes across as a grimdark thing which is hard to justify under scrutiny (much like D-class monthly terminations) unless a contrived situation is set up to make it work.

However, I can briefly suspend my disbelief and not nitpick any further, as this was a great story. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You have a talent for elegantly grim, completely believable scenarios that highlight the sharp edges of the Foundation. The ending to this caught me by surprise, but made perfect sense once it sank in.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was sickening, and awful, makes me want to leave the site forever and never come back. I won't of course, but jesus fucking christ that was the emotional equivalent of an artillery strike.

I'll upvote, but I won't feel good about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fuuck me.
Holy shit.

The Foundation. So cold, but also burning. With stories, with lives. So hopeful, yet so hopeless.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The sense of futility from the portion with the attempted escape is one of those things that'll always get me creeped out.

Most of my nightmares consist of running from people1 at night, and sometimes even in Carolina Beach (this is a real area and I live here, as some of you know). I tried to channel that my-legs-won't-work-fast-enough-help vibe that I get in my dreams. Glad it seems to have worked.

Footnotes
1. Not the Foundation, because I probably wouldn't mind them kidnapping me, and my subconscious is aware of that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well hot damn if that wasn't a punch to the gut. Jesus fuck, Cy. That made me sick to my stomach in the best of ways. +1 <3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This gave me a wonderful sense of dread as I read it, and that ending was a great stinger. The sense of futility from the portion with the attempted escape is one of those things that'll always get me creeped out.

+1

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded: I was taking a run yesterday and the title came to me, so I came back, sat down, and wrote this in five hours. Sometimes these things happen. Thank you to WindyCat, salvagebar, and 9Volt for reading the draft.
